dialogue.utterance: Hi, I'm Taylor, a paramedic with the Rescue Crew. We're here for a cardiac arrest report. Who called 911?
I'm the patient's son. I found her unconscious and not breathing.
Ma'am, can you squeeze my fingers or open your eyes for us, please?
She hasn't responded at all for the past 30 minutes, at least. I've checked thoroughly.
Are you noticing any eye movement, even slight?
She doesn't move or open her eyes. Eyes remain closed and no response from her at all.
Ma'am, if you can hear us-try to make a sound or respond in any way possible, okay?
No response. She's been silent since I found her, and seems completely unresponsive.
Have you observed any movement in her limbs or torso at all?
No. She doesn't move. I've tried several ways to check but got nothing from her.
We're going to initiate ALS care. First, we'll clear and maintain her airway to prevent obstruction.
Go ahead. I'll step back to make room for you. She's not breathing on her own.
We're placing an NPA for airway support and manually maintaining the airway. It appears un-maintainable without intervention.
No chest rise. I don't detect any spontaneous breathing or breath sounds at this point.
We are initiating assisted ventilations with a BVM using an NPA in her right nostril. Positive pressure breathing is in progress now.
Let's feel for a carotid pulse while I work on assisted breathing. You can help me from the left side.
No carotid pulse detected. She's not breathing spontaneously either. Rhythm is flat currently.
Vital signs: Pulse-0. No palpable pulse detected at all, likely due to cardiac arrest as noted in the incident report.
Vital signs: Respiratory rate-0. She doesn't breathe unassisted and remains in apnea at this stage. No spontaneous effort observed.
Vital signs: Blood pressure-0/0. She shows no detectable systolic or diastolic readings. No palpable or measurable blood pressure at this point.
EKG result: Asystole. No electrical activity present. We'll continue resuscitative efforts under the general cardiac arrest protocol.
Vital sign: Blood glucose-203 mg/dL. She is hyperglycemic, a detail noted in pre-EMS efforts prior to our arrival.
Vital sign: SpO2-0%. No oxygen saturation detected and unchanged during initial assessment.
For record purposes, what is your mother's age and gender? Can you tell me just quickly when I ask?
She's 70 years old. Female. I know her well; that's me, her son, and this is her house.
Can you tell me what symptoms you saw before calling 911? What alarmed you most in the moment?
I heard a thud and went to check. Found her unconscious and not breathing. That's what I called for right then and there.
Does she have any known allergies we need to be aware of here, such as foods or medications, even environmental ones?
Yes, she has an allergy to seafood. Specifically, she cannot consume it. Other allergic reactions are unknown.
What is her past medical history? Has she ever had anything major such as surgeries, recurring issues, or chronic conditions you know of?
She has a history of breast cancer and continues to manage chronic pain as part of her treatment.
If you were to rate her pain on a scale from 0 to 10, what would you say? She is unresponsive right now, so this is for documentation only.
Zero. She's unresponsive and not showing any signs of pain at this time-no moaning or grimacing.
Her skin is pink and cool to the touch with normal turgor. She has visible surgical scars on her breasts and abdomen, which may be related to cancer treatment.
Pupils are fixed and non-reactive. Gross visual examination is normal, with no tracheal deviation. Possible signs of laryngeal edema are present.
No head or neck trauma detected at the time I found her. No visible pain or swelling there.
No abnormalities are noted in the ears or mouth. No signs of obstruction or foreign body presence.
Chest is symmetric and atraumatic. No additional findings, no trauma noted so far.
Abdomen is soft and distended with no signs of pain or tenderness upon inspection.
Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) is 3. She shows no response in eye, verbal, or motor categories, indicating severe neurological impairment.
We're using an AED to assess. No activity detected. Could you power it on?
AED shows no rhythm. We are initiating CPR with a manual device and assisted ventilation.
Administering an intraosseous access needle at the left humeral head for immediate medication delivery. It's 45 mm in depth and stained.
We are starting capnography to monitor ETCO2 and confirm airway placement now.
First dose of epinephrine, 1:10,000, 1 mg, is being delivered via intraosseous at this time as per the incident report time.
Following the general cardiac arrest protocol. We'll maintain resuscitative efforts as per standing orders.
Vital sign: Pulse-0. Continuing with no detectable pulse at second check.
Vital sign: Respiratory rate-16 breaths per minute. There is some improvement, but it's shallow, likely due to assisted breathing via the BVM.
Vital signs remain no change at 0/0 for blood pressure.
SpO2 98%-first improvement on oxygen saturation in ongoing re-evaluation read.
Rhythm now is PEA-no pulse with electrical activity. Second rhythm check confirms that.
Switching to the AutoPulse device for more consistent compressions per protocol's recommendation.
Installing four lead monitoring now for ongoing rhythm and heart activity tracking during resuscitation.
Inserting a size 4 King LT/I-Gel airway now with confirmation using capnography post-insertion for accuracy.
Administering second dose of epinephrine at 1 mg via intraosseous as per protocol for resuscitation.
Attempting defibrillation at 120 Joules after detection of ventricular fibrillation now converting back from PEA.
Third round of epinephrine, 1 mg, via intraosseous being administered as per standing orders for resuscitation.
Final rhythm checks show repeated PEA. Continuing to follow standing orders for compressions and care.
Patient has been placed and secured on REEVES stretcher. Manual compressions ongoing throughout transport now.
EMS and RFD are both present and supportive for transport stability. Stretcher locked in position for safety now.
Taking the patient to VCU Medical Center for emergency care. You on board with going to VCU?
VCU is fine. Proceed with that hospital; they know her health history from any prior visits.
Patient is locked in, and the team is ready for transit. No delays or issues expected at this point in transport.
No problems with transport setup found after full review. Communication with hospital ongoing for readiness now.
VCU Medical Center is the predetermined closest and best facility for her current out-of-hospital cardiac arrest situation.
All factors are confirmed and good for departure. Patient is positioned safely for transport to ambulance M185.
Respiratory rate remains stable at 16, per latest measures. No notable change so far detected.
Skin remains normal in tone, texture, and appearance after re-clearance. Scars still visible for documentation purposes.
No signs of trauma further down examination; still functional and intact as per ongoing review.
No jugular venous distention observed, re-checking for potential JVD per initial notes.
What medications is she currently using for daily doses? This helps avoid drug interactions.
She takes Lisinopril, Lorazepam, and Tramadol regularly for blood pressure, chronic pain, and management-those are listed in her chart.
No change noted in clinical status. Full unresponsiveness recorded on Glasgow Coma Scale at 3 again.
EKG shows progression from asystole to PEA and a brief conversion to ventricular fibrillation followed by PEA, as per the time-series.
Final dose of epinephrine is administered and completed prior to entering hospital for continued care from RN/MD.
Following protocol guidelines, we'll conclude with final documentation and handoff to hospital staff.
Patient is secured and ready for transport. Let's confirm one last check for positioning and compressions before transit.
Transport team is in agreement on final clearance. Compressions ongoing and monitors secured to ambulance.
Patient received in VCU Medical Center's EDTA Room 12 after successful transport and delivery. Handing over to receiving team.
Full documentation handed to receiving hospital's RN and MD as part of protocol for patient transfer.
All patient information and current status updated for staff at VCU before offloading. No issues with handoff.
Airway remains secure with the King LT/I-Gel in place, as confirmed by ETCO2 and positive lung sounds earlier.
CV defibrillation was performed at 120 J, per the incident report at 02:14.
No changes to the patient's condition since the last assessment. Transport proceeds as per protocol.
IV access is through intraosseous placement in the left humeral head, all drug delivery confirmed via the IO route.
No jugular venous distention observed. No new findings from re-examination.
Final rhythm check remains PEA. EKG is stable with ongoing monitoring.
Patient transferred with full report. I'll complete this shift from here-thank you both for the team coordination.
VCU Medical Center is the most appropriate hospital for this patient's condition and ongoing care based on the provided protocol.
All team members accounted for and safe. Ambulance ready for departure.
CPR remains manual with no changes to airway, breathing, or placement of the AutoPulse.
Ambulance is cleared for transport. No last-minute issues or delays.
Full交接 completed. No questions or concerns with the patient's current condition or care.
VCU Medical Center is en route with full advance notice and prepared for arrival.
Patient remains in PEA rhythm. Ongoing compressions, assisted breathing, and epinephrine administered up to the third dose.
Initial and final assessments completed. Vitals, medications, and procedures documented with the hospital team.
Transport initiated smoothly with no disruption to compressions or patient monitoring.
All systems checked and working in transit. Ventilation and CPR ongoing with no issues.
All set and ready. Let's go and continue with the resuscitation en route to VCU.
